To earn 1 point, submit all jamovi OMV files in this Computer lab that contain the activities you completed here. The subject of the email should be Stat3 - Comp. lab 11. Send the email to abari.kurzus@gmail.com.

Worked Example 1

Our example database shows customer churn for a European financial services company in 2006. We have a total of 400 rows, each representing a single customer. If the customer closed all of their accounts during the given period, we consider them to have churned, and mark this with a 1 in the corresponding column (has_churned). If the customer still had an open account at the end of the period, the has_churned column contains 0. We will see that instead of using the logical variable that seems obvious at first glance, using 1 and 0 will be much more practical for displaying the figures and throughout the entire process. The two explanatory variables are the time since the customer first purchased a service (time_since_first_purchase) and the time since the customer last purchased a service (time_since_last_purchase). These represent the length of the relationship with the customer and the recency of the customer’s activity, respectively. These two time columns actually contain negative values, as they have been standardized for confidentiality reasons. For more information, see: https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/bayesQR/topics/Churn

The data is available in the file churn.xlsx.

Step 1. Import database

Open the churn.xlsx file in jamovi.

Step 2. Measure type

Please, check the measurement level of the variables. The has_churned variable is categorical, so we set the measurement level to Nominal. The time_since_first_purchase and time_since_last_purchase variables are continuous, so we set the measurement level to Continuous.

Step 3. Binomial Logistic Regression

The aim of this analysis is to predict the probability of customer churn based on the time since the first and last purchase. We will use binomial logistic regression, which is a type of regression analysis used when the dependent variable is binary (i.e., has two possible outcomes). In this case, the dependent variable is has_churned, which can take on values of 0 (not churned) or 1 (churned). The independent variables are time_since_first_purchase and time_since_last_purchase, which are continuous variables. After analyzing the data, we will be able to determine the relationship between these independent variables and the dependent variable. This will allow us to predict the probability of customer churn based on the time since the first and last purchase.

To perform a binomial logistic regression, we need to select the has_churned variable as the dependent variable and the time_since_first_purchase and time_since_last_purchase variables as the independent variables. We can do this by selecting the Regression module from the jamovi menu and then selecting Binomial Logistic Regression. In the dialog box that appears, we can select the dependent and independent variables.

Please, check Collinearity statistics under Assumptions checks and all boxes under Model Fit bar.

Step 4. Interpretation of the results

Model Fit Measures

Measure Value
Deviance 520.9399
AIC 526.9399
BIC 538.9143
  • Deviance, AIC, and BIC are all measures of model fit.
  • Lower values are better, but by themselves they mainly help in comparing different models.
Pseudo R² Value
McFadden’s R² 0.0606
Cox & Snell’s R² 0.0805
Nagelkerke’s R² 0.1074
Tjur’s R² 0.0799
  • These are pseudo-R² values, because logistic regression does not have a true R² like linear regression.
  • They indicate how much variance the model explains:
    • Values between 0.06 and 0.10 suggest a small to moderate effect — the model has some predictive power but isn’t very strong.

Overall Model Test

Statistic Value
Chi-square (χ²) 33.5778
Degrees of freedom (df) 2
p-value < .0001
  • Highly significant model (p < .0001).
  • he predictors together significantly improve the ability to predict has_churned compared to a model with no predictors.

Model Coefficients

Predictor Estimate (log-odds) SE Z p-value
Intercept –0.1168 0.1079 –1.0820 0.2792
time_since_first_purchase –0.6478 0.1391 –4.6553 < .0001
time_since_last_purchase 0.5122 0.1135 4.5136 < .0001

Interpretation: - Intercept is not significant (p = 0.2792) — not important to interpret in logistic regression. - time_since_first_purchase: - Negative coefficient (–0.6478). - he longer the time since the first purchase, the lower the odds of churn. - Significant predictor (p < .0001).

  • time_since_last_purchase:
    • Positive coefficient (0.5122).
    • he longer the time since the last purchase, the higher the odds of churn.
    • Highly significant predictor (p < .0001).

Summary:

  • More recent first purchase = higher risk of churn.
  • More recent last purchase = lower risk of churn.
  • Both time measures significantly predict whether someone will churn.

Assumption Checks

Predictor VIF Tolerance
time_since_first_purchase 1.3228 0.7560
time_since_last_purchase 1.3228 0.7560
  • VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) values are low (around 1.3), much less than 5.
  • *No collinearity problems** between predictors.
  • Tolerance is above 0.2 — also a good sign.

Final Summary

  • The overall logistic regression model is significant.
  • Both predictors are statistically significant in predicting churn.
  • “Time since first purchase” decreases churn risk, while “time since last purchase” increases churn risk.
  • No multicollinearity problems were found.
  • *However, the model’s explained variance is modest** (pseudo-R² ≈ 6–10%).

Final Thought

  • This model works and gives statistically meaningful results,
  • but adding more predictors (e.g., customer satisfaction, support interactions, purchase frequency) might improve the prediction power even further.

Step 5. Odds ratio for good interpretation

To get a better understanding of the results, we can calculate the odds ratios for the coefficients. The odds ratio is a measure of association between an exposure and an outcome. It represents the odds that an outcome will occur given the presence of a particular exposure, compared to the odds of the outcome occurring in the absence of that exposure.

Te get these values, we can check Odds Ratios under Model Fit bar in jamovi.

Interpretation of the Odds Ratios

In logistic regression, odds ratios (OR) help easily interpret the effect of each predictor on the likelihood of the outcome (in your case, has_churned = 1, meaning the customer churned).

Predictor Odds Ratio
Intercept 0.8998
time_since_first_purchase 0.5232
time_since_last_purchase 1.6689

What each Odds Ratio means:

    1. Intercept (OR = 0.8998)
    • Not usually interpreted on its own in logistic regression.
    • It simply represents the baseline odds of churning when both predictors are 0.
    1. time_since_first_purchase (OR = 0.5232)
    • Odds ratio < 1 → the predictor is protective (reduces the odds of churning).
    • Interpretation:
      • For every one-unit increase in time_since_first_purchase, the odds of churning decrease by about 47.7%. (because 1 - 0.5232 = 0.4768 → a 47.7% decrease)
      • Plain English:
      • The longer ago the first purchase was made, the less likely the customer is to churn.
    1. time_since_last_purchase (OR = 1.6689)
    • Odds ratio > 1 → the predictor increases the odds of churning.
    • Interpretation:
      • For every one-unit increase in time_since_last_purchase, the odds of churning increase by about 66.9%. (because 1.6689 - 1 = 0.6689 → a 66.9% increase)
      • Plain English:
      • The longer it has been since the last purchase, the more likely the customer is to churn.

Quick Summary

  • time_since_first_purchase → longer time since first purchase = less risk of churn (protective).
  • time_since_last_purchase → longer time since last purchase = higher risk of churn (risk factor).
  • Odds ratios make it much easier to communicate logistic regression findings to non-technical audiences.

Final Thought

  • Odds ratio < 1 means reduced odds (protective effect).
  • Odds ratio > 1 means increased odds (risk factor).

Both predictors are statistically significant, so their odds ratios are meaningful and should be included in your final report!
